steps.text: Refer to the safety data sheet (SDS) of each specific compound for safe handling instructions.
Close the canister valve.
Record the stop time.
Perform a post-test leak check.
Disconnect the canister from the train.
Confirm the final canister vacuum.
Attach the vacuum gauge to the canister.
Open the canister valve.
Record the final pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume (Eq. 19) to the theoretical volume sampled from the sample time and orifice flow rate (Eq. 20).
Close the valve.
Remove the gauge.
Replace the canister inlet cap.
Perform additional measurements, including stack CO2 (if present), stack moisture, and stack flow.
Collect the required data and minimal information for each sample run.

Determine the retention time and the associated integrated area for unknown peaks in a sample that (1) is not attributed to a target compound or internal standard and (2) the integrated area is greater than or equal to 5% of the integrated peak area of the IS.
Generate unknown mass spectrum.
For each unknown mass spectrum, perform spectral matching to evaluated libraries of reference data (e.g., NIST/EPA/NIH Mass Spectral Library).
For each unknown peak, provide the requested information.
Calculate the Percent Recovery (%RecoveryCCV) using Equation 4: %𝑅𝑒𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑦𝐶𝐶𝑉 = (𝐶𝐶𝐶𝑉 / 𝐶𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑜𝑟𝑒𝑡𝑖𝑐𝑎𝑙) * 100
Calculate the dilution factor from injection (DFI) using Eq. 14: DFI = Vnom / Vinj.
Calculate the concentration, in ppb, of the Target Compound in canister as received Stack Gas (Car) using Eq. 15: Car = CD * DFC * DFI.
Calculate the concentration, in ppb, of the Target Compound in Stack Gas Wet (CSW) using Eq. 16: CSW = Car * (100 - (BWS - 1)) / 100.
Calculate the concentration, in ppb, of the Target Compound in Stack Gas Dry (CD) using the formula: CD = CSW ÷ (100 – BWS)/100.
Calibrate Dynamic Gas Dilution Systems annually.
Calibrate Static Gas Dilution Systems annually.
Perform ICAL analysis of a minimum of five calibration levels.
Analyze Laboratory Blank (LB) using a canister filled with humidified (50% RH) clean diluent gas after high ICAL standard and at the beginning of each analytical sequence.
Analyze MDL Confirmation using a canister of all target VFC prepared at the MDL after each MDL study.
Check for leaks, perform maintenance, and reanalyze ICAL.
Prepare fresh standards and reanalyze ICAL.
Flag any target analytes that are manually integrated in the calibration curve.
Check for instrument leaks and reanalyze LB.
Check instrument for contamination and reanalyze LB.
Prepare a fresh LB and reanalyze.
Reprepare the MDL Confirmation standard.
Reanalyze the MDL study and repeat confirmation.
Prepare fresh standard and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the chromatographic system, repair and reanalyze.
Flag any sample data that does not meet IS retention time criteria.
Normalize all ion abundances to m/z 95.

preconditions.text: Establish safety procedures before using this method.
Become familiar with the safety recommendations in the gas analyzer’s manual.
Ensure sampling equipment is properly cleaned before use.
If CO2 stack concentration is not available, alternative approaches for measuring CO2 must be performed.
If multiple IS are used, the area of the IS nearest in retention time to the unknown peak should be used.
Before sample analysis, annually, after a failing CCV, or when changes/maintenance to the instrument affect calibration response.
If ICAL fails acceptance criteria (each level within ±20% of true value, or average RRF ≤ 20% RSD, or RRTs within 0.06 units from mean RRT).
If Laboratory Blank (LB) fails acceptance criteria (sample analytes >= 3 x MDL or >= 50% of project-required reporting limit).
If MDL Confirmation fails acceptance criteria (primary quantitation ion not detected at 3 times signal to noise or retention time not within ±5 seconds of initial calibration standard).
At startup, annually, or during ICAL.
For each ICAL, LB, CCV, and sample.

constraints.text: Tripping, falling, electrical, and weather safety considerations must all be included in plans to collect source canister samples.
Perform leak checks of the sampling system, ensure proper exhaust ventilation, and practice safe gas cylinder handling to mitigate exposure to harmful compounds.
Exercise extreme care in working with high-pressure gas cylinders.
Avoid exposure to suspected carcinogens or other compounds with serious health risks in all circumstances.
If coeluting compounds have common ions, do not use the common ions as the target’s quantifying or qualifying ions.
Do not use butyl rubber or PFA materials.

If needed for connections or seals, minimize the use of fluoropolymers and fluoroelastomers to avoid adsorption and/or off-gassing of compounds of interest or introduction of other potential interferences.
The pressure should be approximately 17-34 kPa (5-10 in. Hg) vacuum for a 4 to 5 L gas sample collected in the 6 L canister.
An RPD > 20% indicates a potential leak or a clogged orifice and requires resampling.
CO2, if present, can cause analytical interferences and is integral to the stack flow determinations.
The mass spectrum at the peak maximum or averaged across the width of the peak may be used for spectral matching.
If background ions are present, a background subtraction may be performed; however, library matches for both the original and background subtracted spectra must be reported.
Dynamic Gas Dilution Systems must be calibrated against NIST traceable primary flow standards with uncertainties <=0.25%.
Static Gas Dilution Systems pressure transducer(s) must have an accuracy of ± 0.1 % full scale or 0.13 kPa, whichever is smaller.
The lowest calibration concentration must be ≥3x MDL.
Must be within ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
Retention time must be within +/-5 seconds of the most recent calibration.
Ensure BFB Tuning Check Key Ions meet the abundance criteria specified in Table OTM-50-9.
